channel_name: This Week in Startups · Jason Calacanis
video_title: Why the VC Hype Cycle Always Gets It Wrong | VC Roundtable | E2307
gist: A TWiST venture roundtable (host Alex with Ailen Lee/Cowboy VC, Mike Maples/Floodgate, Ben Larair/Lar Hippo) argues that AI-era mega seed and $100M Series A rounds destroy optionality, that returning liquidity now hinges on giants like SpaceX and Anthropic going public, and that durable edge is shifting from generative 'slop' toward 'acceptance AI' (correctness/trust) and model-agnostic architectures as cheap open-weight Chinese models like GLM 5.2 close the gap.

insights.claim: Large institutional LPs need mega-caps like SpaceX, Stripe, OpenAI and Anthropic to go public to recycle capital at scale, so small seed funds face little pressure to return a few million in distributions.
insights.speaker: Ben Larair, Lar Hippo
insights.so_what: Watch the SpaceX/Stripe/OpenAI/Anthropic IPO pipeline as the real unlock for institutional venture liquidity, not smaller seed-fund exits.
insights.quote: they need the you know SpaceX's and Stripes and Open AIS and anthropics to go public to sort of feed money back into their system in large scale

insights.claim: One Cowboy VC LP with a 25% target venture allocation has drifted to 45% exposure because of appreciation in large fund holdings, so upcoming SpaceX distributions should free them to recommit to venture.
insights.speaker: Ailen Lee, Cowboy VC
insights.so_what: The denominator effect is capping new LP commitments; distributions from mega-holdings could re-open allocations to both large and small funds.
insights.quote: they had a target venture exposure of 25%, but because there's been so much appreciation from some of the larger fund holdings, they're at 45% venture exposure

insights.claim: The current wave of venture markups echoes the 2021 'denominator effect' of paper gains, and it is unproven whether these gains will have more staying power than the 2021 ones that reversed.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Treat 2026 fund NAVs as paper until DPI shows up; the 2021 comparison is a caution against extrapolating markups.
insights.quote: we're back to sort of like the 21 denominator effect. paper gains that hopefully turn into not paper gains

insights.claim: Floodgate returned a little over $350 million to LPs in the last two years, arguing seed funds have more exit optionality than mega-funds and can proactively harvest liquidity.
insights.speaker: Mike Maples, Floodgate
insights.so_what: Small seed funds can generate DPI independent of the IPO window, a differentiator when courting LPs starved for distributions.
insights.quote: we we've returned a little over 350 million in the last two years

insights.claim: Bending Spoons' IPO priced at $29 per share (above its $26-28 range) for roughly $18.5B non-diluted versus an $11B last valuation, with an S1 showing doubled year-over-year revenue and positive GAAP operating and net income in Q1.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: A profitable rollup of aging software assets (AOL, Evernote, Vimeo) IPO-ing well validates the acquire-and-optimize model as a liquidity path for 2000s-era unicorns.
insights.quote: priced at $29 per share, up from its range of 26 to 28, worth about 18.5 billion non-diluted, last valued at 11 billion

insights.claim: Rollup vehicles like Bending Spoons and PE firms are becoming the exit route for older 'pre-AI' SaaS companies trading at poor multiples, and M&A is expected to be even more active in 2027.
insights.speaker: Mike Maples, Floodgate
insights.so_what: Position pre-AI portfolio companies for rollup/PE acquisition; expect an M&A recovery through 2027 as buyers get acquisitive again.
insights.quote: it's exciting that M&A is coming back and I assume that 27 will be will be maybe even more active

insights.claim: Floodgate's Ignite (Venit Jane) sold to private equity in February 2025 for about $1.5 billion, and in hindsight that looked smart given the subsequent collapse in SaaS multiples, since being public means one missed quarter gets a nearly-20-year-old company 'eviscerated.'
insights.speaker: Mike Maples, Floodgate
insights.so_what: For profitable-but-slow SaaS, a private/PE sale can beat a punishing public-market listing where a single miss is fatal.
insights.quote: you miss one quarter and it it's like you just get eviscerated

insights.claim: The operating philosophy is that 'companies get bought not sold,' and Lar Hippo (via Eric Hippo) holds that a company's first serious offer is probably its best offer, so non-extraordinary companies should not be dismissive or greedy when a real approach appears.
insights.speaker: Ben Larair, Lar Hippo
insights.so_what: Liquidity windows for average companies are rare; take genuine acquisition interest seriously rather than holding out for a better multiple.
insights.quote: companies get bought not sold

themes.theme: Reviving pre-AI companies & burning the boats

insights.claim: Seed investors are being forced to re-engage with 8-to-12-year-old portfolio companies because later-stage investors at newly enormous funds have high turnover and are asleep at the wheel on dysfunctional boards.
insights.speaker: Ben Larair, Lar Hippo
insights.so_what: Governance vacuums at older startups create an opening for small early holders to force AI transitions; also a diligence flag on which boards are functional.
insights.quote: we have some sort of pretty dysfunctional boards with real companies, but a bunch of people asleep at the wheel

insights.claim: Founders now check references by asking how a VC behaved during a downturn, making reputation for standing by companies in hard times a competitive edge in winning deals.
insights.speaker: Ailen Lee, Cowboy VC
insights.so_what: Post-ZIRP, downside support is a differentiator VCs can market; founders weight it in competitive rounds.
insights.quote: they will ask founders like how did they handle a downturn? How did they handle things when things weren't good?

insights.claim: Cowboy portfolio company Mutiny 'burned the boats,' cutting staff and rebuilding an entirely new AI-native product suite rather than incrementally tweaking its prior product, and believes it could not have gotten there otherwise.
insights.speaker: Ailen Lee, Cowboy VC
insights.so_what: A concrete case that full AI-native rebuilds (not tweaks) may be required for pre-AI apps to stay relevant.

insights.claim: Intercom rebranded to Finn as an AI-first restart and sold for a quick number, cited as a successful 'burn the boats' AI pivot.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Rebrand-and-restart is a viable, sometimes lucrative path for incumbents forced to become AI-native.

insights.claim: Mike Maples' 'rule of 70' discipline (at breakeven grow 70%; at 40% growth carry 30% margins) turned Keepsafe into a profit-first dividend machine, returning Floodgate over $10 million on a roughly $1.5 million investment via profit dividends.
insights.speaker: Mike Maples, Floodgate
insights.so_what: A profit-first operating rule can produce strong cash-on-cash returns for companies without a venture-scale growth path.
insights.quote: if we're at break even we've got to grow 70% a year. If we're growing 40% a year, we got to have 30% margins

insights.claim: Maples frames a startup's life as a sequence (0-to-1 insight, product-market fit, 1-to-X, predictable growth justifying burn, profitable growth, profitable decline), and says a VC's job is to locate the founder in that sequence because each stage has its own 'laws of gravity' on how much growth must justify burn.
insights.speaker: Mike Maples, Floodgate
insights.so_what: A diagnostic for whether a company's burn is justified by its stage-appropriate growth rate.

themes.theme: Growth bar, fund size & capital discipline

insights.claim: The AI-era growth bar to raise a Series A or B has jumped from the old best-in-class 'triple triple double double' to roughly 1-to-5 (or 1-to-4.5) then 5-to-20, so founders must quintuple/quadruple rather than triple to stay fundable in 2026-2027.
insights.speaker: Ailen Lee, Cowboy VC
insights.so_what: Benchmark for A/B fundability has reset dramatically higher; founders growing 'only' triple risk being un-fundable.
insights.quote: it's probably one to five uh or one to four and a half depending on what you're doing and then probably five to 20

insights.claim: The high-growth bar is starving capital from harder, moat-building businesses and forcing money into companies solving throwaway problems that exist only for the next days, generating easy-come easy-go revenue.
insights.speaker: Ben Larair, Lar Hippo
insights.so_what: Contrarian edge exists in funding harder, defensible bets 'into the void' that Sand Hill won't follow-on; also a warning on the fragility of fast AI revenue.
insights.quote: they're solving a problem that is like a problem that exists for the next 11 days and raising a bunch of money

insights.claim: Consensus seed rounds are getting done at $50-70 million valuations, which the panel views as a sign of froth.
insights.speaker: Ben Larair, Lar Hippo
insights.so_what: Elevated seed entry prices compress future returns and raise the bar for the next round's markup.
insights.quote: consensus seed rounds getting done at 50 and 60 and 70 million valuation

insights.claim: Maples treats fund size as strategy and a commitment to LPs about the largest exit: for a 5x fund, the best exit's profit must be about 2.5x the fund, so a $100 million fund needs a roughly $250 million exit.
insights.speaker: Mike Maples, Floodgate
insights.so_what: A quantitative test of whether a fund's ownership and exit math can actually deliver its target multiple.
insights.quote: if you have a $100 million fund, your best exit needs to be 250 million if you're going to have a 5x fund

insights.claim: Maples divides deals into 'hot' projects (multi-stage darlings, e.g., teams peeling out of Anthropic/OpenAI, where the non-consensus is that upside is even bigger than believed) and 'weird' projects off the multi-stage radar that a seed fund must back alone.
insights.speaker: Mike Maples, Floodgate
insights.so_what: Different playbooks per deal type: work your way into hot deals relying on multi-stage de-risking; be prepared to go it alone on weird ones.
insights.quote: it's not that nobody thinks it's exciting. It's that it's even more exciting than you thought it was

insights.claim: Floodgate's Smarterdx (led by Ann Mirao, invested 2022) exited for about $1 billion, a great outcome for a seed fund but immaterial to a multi-stage fund, illustrating why 'weird' deals suit small funds.
insights.speaker: Mike Maples, Floodgate
insights.so_what: A $1B exit that moves a seed fund but not a mega-fund shows why exit math should drive which deals each fund size pursues.

insights.claim: Lar Hippo deliberately refuses to raise a much bigger fund (funds grew from $145M to $200M) because a $300M fund can't solve early-stage companies' problems and Series B leading is a fundamentally different skill set.
insights.speaker: Ben Larair, Lar Hippo
insights.so_what: Staying small is a deliberate strategy; fund-size discipline signals where a firm's edge actually is.

themes.theme: Mega seed / $100M Series A rounds & king-making

insights.claim: A wave of nine-figure Series A rounds is underway, including StarCloud's $170M, General Intuition's roughly $320M, Scale Cognition's $100M, and Scout AI's $100M, at sizes and valuations that no longer match the traditional seed/A stage.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: The 'seed'/'A' labels now mask growth-stage economics; founders raising them are held to $50-100M-round-lead traction expectations.

insights.claim: Founders raising a huge seed at, say, $120M post lock themselves into needing a $200-300M next round with real traction and customers, so the label 'seed' or 'A' understates the metrics they'll be held accountable to.
insights.speaker: Ailen Lee, Cowboy VC
insights.so_what: Oversized early valuations create markup pressure that forces premature scaling; a caution for founders chasing headline rounds.
insights.quote: I have never seen more people who started a business 3 weeks ago and have decided that they're going to raise a $20 million to get down to 100 pre

insights.claim: Multi-stage firms are 'king-making' by writing the first large check in competitive categories to signal rivals to stay away, sometimes anointing two or three 'kings' per category, but the signal does not always work.
insights.speaker: Ben Larair, Lar Hippo
insights.so_what: A first big check can be a competitive-signaling tactic rather than proof of a winner; discount the anointment.
insights.quote: they they want to communicate something to the market which is stay away watch out we've got this

insights.claim: There have been no blowups yet among companies that raised $100M+ Series A rounds, but within 12-36 months the rubber meets the road and some companies briefly worth tens of billions will go to zero.
insights.speaker: Ben Larair, Lar Hippo
insights.so_what: Expect high-profile AI startup failures on a 1-3 year horizon; the speed of value creation implies symmetric speed of collapse.
insights.quote: you are going to have companies that are worth tens of billions of dollars that also go to zero

insights.claim: No company has ever had a greater-than-$10B exit after raising that much in its seed round; the largest seed round Maples can find with an exit that size is Whiz at $21 million, so mega seed rounds destroy optionality.
insights.speaker: Mike Maples, Floodgate
insights.so_what: Historical base rate argues against giant seed rounds if the goal is a generational outcome; they eliminate down-round survival paths.
insights.quote: The the biggest seed round I can find on record with a exit that size is whiz and it was $21 million

insights.claim: Maples argues that if you believe we are in a bubble, raising a giant round is the last thing you should do because it forfeits the optionality to profit across a wide range of scenarios, drawing a dotcom parallel that Mark Cuban's broadcast.com would be forgotten had he raised $100M in seed.
insights.speaker: Mike Maples, Floodgate
insights.so_what: In a bubble, capital-light positioning preserves exit optionality; the dotcom pattern shows over-raising at peak prices forced give-backs and shutdowns.
insights.quote: if if your goal is to create generational wealth and you believe we're in a bubble, this is the last thing you would do

insights.claim: Startups are turning to cheaper open-weight models like GLM 5.2 partly because the government is precluding access to the latest Anthropic and OpenAI models, with portfolio companies reporting equivalent results at a fraction of the cost.
insights.speaker: Ailen Lee, Cowboy VC
insights.so_what: Open-weight Chinese models are becoming a real cost lever; the 'GLM moment' follows the DeepSeek moment as pressure on frontier-lab pricing.
insights.quote: they're getting equivalent results for a fraction of the cost

insights.claim: Fine-tuning open models is a poor use of time and money because the base models improve so fast (e.g., Kimmy K 2.5 to 2.6 to 2.7, like Cursor's Composer 2 and 2.5) that any tuned advantage is erased within about a month.
insights.speaker: Ailen Lee, Cowboy VC
insights.so_what: Argues against heavy fine-tuning investment; build to swap models rather than customize a fast-obsolescing one.
insights.quote: you could spend a lot of time and money fine-tuning something and how long is that gonna buy you? A month.

insights.claim: Because every startup's long-term model assumes token prices fall continuously, frontier labs (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google) are 'scary' as they push into the application layer, making the app layer a finicky place to invest where infra and application boundaries are unclear.
insights.speaker: Ben Larair, Lar Hippo
insights.so_what: Application-layer bets carry platform-encroachment risk; the labs' own move down-stack is the key threat to app-layer defensibility.
insights.quote: everyone's long-term business model is predicated on token prices going down and down and down and down and down

insights.claim: Maples argues that as AI makes generated output abundant, the scarce and valuable thing becomes 'acceptance AI' - correctness and proof of correctness from a credibly neutral third party, analogous to audit firms and to why Floodgate backed Okta for identity.
insights.speaker: Mike Maples, Floodgate
insights.so_what: Points investors toward neutral verification/trust layers (like Drata) as the durable application-layer opportunity amid AI 'slop.'
insights.quote: if slop is abundant then you start to ask well what's scarce and I think what's scarce is correctness and proof of correctness

insights.claim: Ann Mirao's 'AI-pilled' framework distinguishes using the best/most expensive models for compounding competitive-advantage loops (customer feedback, new product ideas, A/B testing) from the cheaper models that go into a shipped product's bill of materials, so companies create knowledge with frontier models and transfer it with cheaper ones.
insights.speaker: Mike Maples, Floodgate
insights.so_what: A model-tiering strategy: frontier models for internal edge, cheap/open models for product delivery; Applied Intuition cited as AI-pilled in operations.
insights.quote: how do you how do you turn a deep work discovery into a checklist manifesto deliverable?

insights.claim: Maurice Rosen's substack 'The End of Decisions' argues AI is the first general-purpose reasoning layer that can act as a solver for historically qualitative domains, implying that marginal intelligence gains in each new frontier model are highly valuable when the model effectively runs a business's decisions.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: If true, high-level decision-making becomes the premium use case that justifies the most expensive models and expands their market.
insights.quote: the first general purpose reasoning layer that can start to function as a solver for domains that have historically been too qualitative for software

insights.claim: A durable opportunity exists in routing and eval infrastructure that lets companies decide which product functions need frontier models versus cheaper open-source ones, and firms will have to choose whether to build that infrastructure themselves or buy it.
insights.speaker: Ailen Lee, Cowboy VC
insights.so_what: Model routing and evaluation is an emerging infra category as multi-model, cost-optimized stacks become standard.

insights.claim: If an application company owns neither the routing, the model, the compute, nor proprietary customer data, it has no defensibility - so owning customer data is the minimum requirement to be more than a non-defensible wrapper.
insights.speaker: Ben Larair, Lar Hippo
insights.so_what: A defensibility checklist for app-layer diligence: routing, model, compute, or (at minimum) proprietary data.
insights.quote: If they don't own the routing, they didn't build the model, they're not doing the compute, they don't have the customer data, then what the are they for?

insights.claim: AI-infrastructure funding is surging, with Etched raising $800M for a transformer-specific ASIC, Lightmatter raising $850M, and DG Matrix raising $20M for solid-state transformers, alongside a crop of data-center cooling startups.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Specialized AI silicon and power/cooling hardware are drawing very large rounds; a map of data-center needs marks the hot infra sub-sectors.

insights.claim: Data-center power demand is a direct tailwind for suppliers like Bloom Energy (Ailen Lee's former Kleiner portfolio company) and memory makers like Micron, whose earnings the host urges people to read.
insights.speaker: Ailen Lee, Cowboy VC
insights.so_what: Power and memory are downstream AI-compute beneficiaries; Micron's earnings are flagged as a striking read on memory demand.
insights.quote: look at Micron's numbers. It's incredible

insights.claim: Bedrock Robotics is building 'Waymo for diggers,' an autonomy play for construction equipment that the host calls defensible because it targets a huge industry with almost no competitors and little venture attention.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Overlooked physical-world verticals (construction autonomy) can offer defensibility precisely because tech investors ignore them.
insights.quote: they're building essentially whimo for diggers

insights.claim: Drata, at a roughly $2 billion valuation, is positioned as a neutral trust/compliance layer that will help enterprises monitor the trustworthiness of AI agents, an evolution of its existing customer relationships rather than a rebuild - and Maples regrets missing it.
insights.speaker: Ailen Lee, Cowboy VC
insights.so_what: Agent monitoring/compliance from a neutral third party (not the model vendor) is framed as a durable enterprise category.
insights.quote: you want to have a trusted third party um that isn't your current vendor that's trying to use all your data

insights.claim: Lar Hippo's Board (by Brin Putnham, who built Mirror and sold it to Lululemon) is a physical digital board-game device with 'stunning' high-regularity usage data, funded despite children's games being deeply out of favor, with USV leading the last round.
insights.speaker: Ben Larair, Lar Hippo
insights.so_what: Contrarian consumer hardware with strong retention can get funded even in an out-of-favor category; usage data is the key validation signal.

insights.claim: Ben Larair is 'open for business' in consumer but still cannot find a differentiated application-layer breakout, seeing mostly lightweight wrappers with excuses for why they are not wrappers, and finds agents built on everyone else's infra unexciting.
insights.speaker: Ben Larair, Lar Hippo
insights.so_what: Consumer AI application layer still lacks a defensible breakout; wrapper economics remain the trap to avoid.
insights.quote: We see a bunch of lightweight rappers that you know have a bunch of explanations for why they're not lightweight rappers that are still lightweight rappers

insights.claim: Each year has a hot theme (2013 wearables, 2018 VR, 2019 scooters) but the best companies actually founded in those years never match the theme - Anthropic was founded when crypto/web3 was hot and Whiz when 'future of work' was the narrative.
insights.speaker: Ailen Lee, Cowboy VC
insights.so_what: The reason the VC hype cycle 'gets it wrong': the enduring winners are usually off-theme and take 3-5 years of obscurity to emerge.
insights.quote: Anthropic was when crypto was really hot in web three. Whiz was when we were talking about future of work

insights.claim: Cowboy favors 'off Broadway' founders without perfect pedigree, noting Databricks' founders were academics and open-source contributors - a non-consensus bet at the time even if it reads as pedigreed today.
insights.speaker: Ailen Lee, Cowboy VC
insights.so_what: Edge may lie in backing non-pedigreed founders with unique insight rather than crowded central-casting profiles.

insights.claim: Maples would avoid backing AI productivity or agentic workflow tools that lack a built-in increasing-returns or network-effect mechanism, because such features can simply appear in Sam Altman's next demo.
insights.speaker: Mike Maples, Floodgate
insights.so_what: Screen agentic/productivity startups for a core increasing-returns mechanism; without it they are exposed to being absorbed by a frontier lab's next release.
insights.quote: I don't know why Sam Alman's not going to have that in his next demo

themes.theme: AI regulation, geopolitics & the Mythos/Fable episode

insights.claim: Maples declines to grade the Trump administration's handling of the Mythos and Fable model situation but warns that frontier models attracting government attention risks the government 'backdooring' its way into AI regulation, which he considers bad for US competitiveness against China.
insights.speaker: Mike Maples, Floodgate
insights.so_what: Regulatory-encroachment risk on frontier AI is a policy overhang; hyperscalers (Amazon singled out) are seen as not helping their own case.
insights.quote: I get nervous about the government sort of backdooring its way into regulating AI

insights.claim: The panel frames AI as a national-security cold war where US federal institutions and companies are not ready for adversaries hacking systems 24/7 with agents, and unlike the Manhattan Project the US is not clearly ahead, with GLM 5.2 reportedly roughly commensurate with Fable or Mythos.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Chinese open-weight models closing to near-frontier parity undercuts the case for US export/access restrictions and raises agent-driven cyber risk.
insights.quote: China's really banging on. I mean, we all saw the GLM 5.2 headline about how they think that it's going to be roughly commensurate with maybe Fable, maybe mythos

insights.claim: Crunchbase's value rests on hard-to-source proprietary private-company data, which the host (a shareholder) and Ailen Lee argue is scarce and valuable because models cannot easily surface private-company information.
insights.speaker: Ailen Lee, Cowboy VC
insights.so_what: Proprietary private-market data is a defensible moat in an AI world where public data is commoditized.
insights.quote: private company data is one of the hardest things to find out about

themes.theme: AI backlash, inequality & the labor share

insights.claim: The host cites a FRED chart showing labor compensation as a share of GDP falling from the high 60s in the 1950s to about 57% today, framing it as the root of rising public discontent that AI is unlikely to improve in the short or medium term.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: The declining labor share quantifies the inequality driving anti-AI/anti-data-center sentiment - a political risk to the AI buildout.
insights.quote: share of labor comp as a percentage of GDP... all the way down to about 57

insights.claim: Larair argues AI is viewed very negatively by people outside the industry, the wealth gap is worsening 'by the minute,' and he distrusts the government to regulate AI even as it is a national-security cold war - a tension he sees ending 'terribly' absent better pie-sharing.
insights.speaker: Ben Larair, Lar Hippo
insights.so_what: Social license is a real risk to AI; visible in data-center protests, it could become an electoral issue if inequality is not addressed.
insights.quote: the wealth gap is only getting worse by the minute in a way that is like I don't know how this ends anything other than terribly

insights.claim: The panel argues the pro-market 'adult in the room' position on AI has no political home, with the left moving to Democratic socialism and the right to anti-immigrant MAGA, so proponents cannot get a hearing.
insights.speaker: Mike Maples, Floodgate
insights.so_what: A political vacuum around pro-AI, free-market arguments raises the odds of poorly-designed policy responses.
insights.quote: if you're a free market capitalist wanting to make a pro common sense argument for it, you have no home

insights.claim: California's proposed billionaire tax and similar brute-force measures are seen as band-aids that drive wealth out, with the host already talking to founders relocating to Nevada to escape the tax.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Aggressive state wealth taxes risk accelerating founder/capital flight rather than fixing inequality.
insights.quote: everyone's going to leave. I'm already talking to factors who live in Nevada just across the border because they want to get away from the tax

insights.claim: Ailen Lee argues the absence of social-media regulation harmed a generation of kids and that AI's security and societal ramifications are far more grave, implying earlier oversight would have been warranted.
insights.speaker: Ailen Lee, Cowboy VC
insights.so_what: The social-media regret is used as an argument for at least some proactive guardrails on AI's societal impact.
insights.quote: I certainly wish looking back that we had had more regulation of social media
